Notes: Status: wild naturalized and cultivated. Tastiness: opinions are mixed or it is only good in certain preparations. Flavor: earthy, sweet, artichoke-like root. Best prep: dig first-year roots; peel and simmer, roast, or stir-fry; blanch peeled stalks. Cautions: second-year roots get woody; burrs spread easily.

Status: wild native and cultivated. Tastiness: opinions are mixed or it is only good in certain preparations. Flavor: peppery greens and mildly sweet root. Best prep: use first-year roots cooked; harvest young greens before they toughen. Notes: Often persists by reseeding in open ground..

Status: cultivated and wild naturalized. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: sweet, nutty, carrot-like. Best prep: dig after frost for sweeter roots; roast, mash, or add to soups. Cautions: foliage and sap can cause phytophotodermatitis.

Status: cultivated and wild naturalized. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: mild, sweet, oyster-like root. Best prep: dig in the first year; peel roots and cook gently or use young shoots.

Status: wild naturalized. Tastiness: mainly considered useful rather than notably tasty. Flavor: carroty when very young, then fibrous. Best prep: use only first-year roots while small; seeds can be used as a spice. Cautions: dangerous lookalikes include poison hemlock.
Status: wild naturalized and cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: mild, juicy, corn-silk-like. Best prep: best fresh in salads, sandwiches, or lightly wilted.

Status: wild naturalized. Tastiness: opinions are mixed or it is only good in certain preparations. Flavor: mild and mucilaginous. Best prep: best mixed into soups, sauteed greens, or used young in salads. Cautions: texture can be slippery if overused.
Status: cultivated and wild naturalized. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: mild, nutty, tender. Best prep: harvest whole rosettes in cool weather for salads.
Status: cultivated and wild naturalized. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: peppery, nutty. Best prep: best young in salads or quickly wilted. Cautions: older leaves get much hotter.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: mild to pleasantly bitter depending on strain. Best prep: use volunteers young; quality varies because seedlings may not match the parent strain. Cautions: volunteers can revert or vary in quality.
Status: cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: mild, spinach-like, slightly salty. Best prep: use as cut-and-come-again greens or wilt larger leaves.

Harvest window: Spring leaves; spring flowers; early summer pods
Notes: Status: wild naturalized. Tastiness: opinions are mixed or it is only good in certain preparations. Flavor: garlicky, mustardy, sometimes bitter with age. Best prep: use very young leaves for pesto or saute; flowers and tender pods are milder than mature leaves. Cautions: highly invasive in eastern woodlands.
Status: wild naturalized. Tastiness: opinions are mixed or it is only good in certain preparations. Flavor: peppery, sharp. Best prep: use sparingly raw like cress or blend into herb sauces.

Status: wild naturalized and cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: mild, spinach-like. Best prep: use young leaves fresh or cooked; seeds can be cleaned and cooked like a pseudograin. Cautions: contains oxalates; excess nitrates can accumulate in rich soils.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: mild, spinach-like. Best prep: harvest repeatedly as cut-and-come-again greens; older leaves cook best. Cautions: contains oxalates.

Status: cultivated and wild naturalized. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: mild, succulent, slightly sweet. Best prep: best fresh as salad greens while plants are tender. Cautions: fades quickly in heat.

Status: wild naturalized. Tastiness: opinions are mixed or it is only good in certain preparations. Flavor: green, earthy, similar to stronger spinach. Best prep: use young leaves cooked or wilted; seeds can be toasted or porridge-cooked. Cautions: nitrates can accumulate in stressed or heavily manured plants.

Status: wild naturalized. Tastiness: opinions are mixed or it is only good in certain preparations. Flavor: peppery, cress-like. Best prep: use young rosettes raw or cooked; pods can be used as a spicy garnish.

Status: wild naturalized. Tastiness: mainly considered useful rather than notably tasty. Flavor: very bitter. Best prep: only the youngest leaves are worthwhile; best mixed with milder greens. Cautions: milky sap and bitterness limit use.

Status: wild naturalized. Tastiness: opinions are mixed or it is only good in certain preparations. Flavor: mustardy, sometimes bitter. Best prep: use early spring leaves and buds before flowering for the best flavor. Cautions: older leaves become bitter.

Status: wild naturalized and cultivated. Tastiness: opinions are mixed or it is only good in certain preparations. Flavor: strong mustard heat. Best prep: use young greens cooked or raw in small amounts; seeds can be used as spice. Cautions: can self-sow aggressively.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: opinions are mixed or it is only good in certain preparations. Flavor: resinous petals, mild to bitter leaves. Best prep: use petals fresh or dried as a garnish or colorant; harvest leaves very young. Cautions: main value is culinary color and repeated bloom.
Status: cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: mild mustard bite. Best prep: cut repeatedly while young; flowering stems are also good cooked.

Edible parts / uses: Leaves; flower shoots; young seed pods
Notes: Status: cultivated and wild naturalized. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: mustardy and pungent. Best prep: cut repeatedly while young; flower shoots are good lightly cooked.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: peppery, watercress-like. Best prep: use fresh leaves and flowers, or pickle the green seeds as faux capers. Cautions: volunteer behavior depends on winter and soil exposure.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty. Flavor: peppery roots and leaves; crisp pods. Best prep: volunteer roots are best harvested small; pods are often the best use from self-sown plants. Cautions: roots turn woody fast in heat.

Edible parts / uses: Leaves; flower buds; young seed pods
Notes: Status: wild naturalized. Tastiness: opinions are mixed or it is only good in certain preparations. Flavor: mustardy, peppery. Best prep: harvest before flowering for milder greens; buds cook like tiny broccoli. Cautions: older plants become fibrous and hot.

Status: wild naturalized. Tastiness: mainly considered useful rather than notably tasty. Flavor: peppery and often coarse. Best prep: use only the youngest greens and tender pods. Cautions: roots are usually not worthwhile.

Status: cultivated and wild naturalized. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty in culinary use. Flavor: cucumber-like. Best prep: use flowers fresh and leaves very young or cooked. Cautions: older leaves are hairy and coarse.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty in culinary use. Flavor: classic celery flavor. Best prep: volunteers are often best used for leaf celery, soup greens, and seed rather than thick blanching stalks. Cautions: wild-type volunteers can be stringier than selected cultivars.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty in culinary use. Flavor: delicate anise-parsley flavor. Best prep: best cut young in cool weather and used fresh. Cautions: fades quickly in heat.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: widely regarded as highly tasty in its culinary role. Flavor: bright, citrusy, herbal. Best prep: use leaves young, green seeds fresh, and dry seeds as coriander spice. Notes: A classic self-sowing herb in cool-season windows..

Edible parts / uses: Leaves; flowers; green seeds; dry seeds
Notes: Status: cultivated. Tastiness: widely regarded as highly tasty in its culinary role. Flavor: fresh, feathery, aromatic. Best prep: harvest leaves repeatedly and allow some umbels to mature for reseeding and spice use.

Edible parts / uses: Leaves; stems; roots in some forms
Notes: Status: cultivated. Tastiness: widely regarded as highly tasty in its culinary role. Flavor: clean, green, celery-like. Best prep: cut outer stems repeatedly; let some second-year plants seed to maintain the patch. Cautions: second-year plants turn coarse once flowering advances.

Status: cultivated and wild naturalized. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty in culinary use. Flavor: distinctive cinnamon-basil-mint note. Best prep: use fresh leaves, wraps, pickles, or tempura; harvest before plants get tough. Cautions: can reseed aggressively in some gardens.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty in culinary use. Flavor: rye-bread, warm spice flavor. Best prep: use spring leaves fresh, first-year roots cooked, and second-year seeds dried.
Status: cultivated and wild naturalized. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty in culinary use. Flavor: apple-like floral tea. Best prep: pick flowers frequently and dry promptly for tea.
Status: cultivated. Tastiness: generally regarded as tasty in culinary use. Flavor: onion-oregano-pepper spice note. Best prep: let pods dry fully, then thresh seeds for spice use.

Status: wild naturalized and cultivated. Tastiness: mainly considered useful rather than notably tasty. Flavor: pea-like shoots, mild flowers. Best prep: use only young tips or flowers rather than mature dry seed. Cautions: mature seeds are not a preferred staple human food.

Status: cultivated and wild naturalized. Tastiness: mainly considered useful rather than notably tasty. Flavor: mild, green, slightly sweet blossoms. Best prep: use young leaves sparingly and flowers in teas or salads. Cautions: mostly a cover crop with minor food use.

Status: wild naturalized and cultivated. Tastiness: mainly a survival food or only marginally palatable. Flavor: minor, green, pea-like. Best prep: if used at all, keep to small amounts of very young shoots or flowers. Cautions: grown mainly as a cover crop; not usually treated as a human food staple.

Status: cultivated. Tastiness: opinions are mixed or it is only good in certain preparations. Flavor: nutty seeds, spinach-like leaves. Best prep: cut young leaves freely or let seedheads mature and thresh for grain. Cautions: grain harvest is more laborious than leaf use.

Status: cultivated and wild naturalized. Tastiness: widely regarded as highly tasty. Flavor: nutty seeds and artichoke-like buds. Best prep: dry heads thoroughly for seeds; immature buds can be steamed like small artichokes. Cautions: seed quality varies in volunteers from hybrid stock.

Status: wild naturalized and cultivated. Tastiness: widely regarded as highly tasty. Flavor: lemony, juicy, slightly salty. Best prep: use fresh in salads, quick pickles, or brief sautees while stems are tender. Cautions: can spread densely in hot beds and paths.
